title: Cosolvency response in polymer brushes
abstract: We present the first analytic theory with elegant and closed-form analytical solutions to explore the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, where polymer chains that are poorly soluble in two pure solvents become fully soluble in certain mixtures thereof. This effect is key to designing stimulus-responsive smart materials but has not previously been addressed by analytic theory for polymer brushes. Our theoretical framework reveals that preferential adsorption of cosolvent induces an effective repulsion between monomers solvated by cosolvent and those solvated by solvent. The equilibrium solvation of polymer chains by cosolvent gives rise to a concentration-dependent $χ$-function, which captures the effective interactions within the brush and reproduces the reentrant behavior characteristic of the cosolvency effect. The model predicts a discontinuous soluble transition followed by a re-collapse transition at higher cosolvent concentrations. Analytical treatment within a minimal free-energy model for the case of two symmetric poor solvents shows that the swelling and re-collapse transitions share the same thermodynamic origin. For low-density brushes, we derive an analytical approximation and delineate the phase diagram of parameter space in which discontinuous transitions occur. For cosolvency to take place, the theory specifies a minimum strength for preferential solvation and the associated repulsive coupling. Furthermore, it demonstrates that, contrary to previous models, repulsive interactions between cosolvent and solvent in the bulk are not required. This work lays the groundwork for the rational design of smart stimulus-responsive materials based on the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, a capability which was not previously established.
summary: This paper provides the first analytical framework to describe the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes. It demonstrates that the phenomenon is driven by an effective repulsion between monomers solvated by different solvents, triggered by preferential adsorption. The theory predicts a complex phase behavior including a discontinuous swelling transition followed by a re-collapse. Notably, it shows that repulsive interactions between the two solvents in the bulk are not a prerequisite for cosolvency. This work offers a theoretical foundation for designing smart, stimulus-responsive materials.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of an analytic theory to explain the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, specifically how polymer chains that are poorly soluble in two pure solvents become soluble in certain mixtures.
main_result: The authors develop the first analytic theory with closed-form solutions, revealing that preferential adsorption of a cosolvent induces an effective repulsion between different types of solvated monomers, driving a discontinuous soluble transition and subsequent re-collapse.
method: A mean-field free-energy model based on an extension of Flory-Huggins theory is used, employing perturbation expansions and Maxwell construction to determine phase boundaries.
model_or_system: The system consists of polymer brushes (chains end-anchored to a surface) immersed in a binary mixture of two poor solvents. The model incorporates preferential adsorption, effective monomer-monomer repulsion, and a concentration-dependent chi-function.
key_observables: Brush thickness, monomer volume fraction, effective Flory-Huggins parameter (chi-function), and phase transition boundaries (spinodal and binodal).
important_parameters: Preferential-solvation strength (epsilon_1), repulsion coupling (epsilon_2), grafting density (sigma), solvent demixing tendency (chi_s), and chemical potential (mu).
main_assumptions: The theory assumes a homogeneous density profile (Alexander-de Gennes approach), an incompressible system, and identical sizes for monomers and solvent molecules.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of preferential solvation; Figure 2 plots the square of the external adsorption field vs. monomer density to show the effect of repulsion and grafting density; Figure 3 shows the phase transition conditions; Figure 5 presents the derived phase diagram; Figure 6 illustrates the free energy density and the role of the third virial coefficient.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the cosolvency problem, presents a mean-field free-energy framework, derives analytical solutions for the solvation and phase transitions, explores specific regimes like low grafting density and symmetric solvents, and concludes with a phase diagram and discussion of implications for material design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
